title: SEGMENT 1 — The brakes the cancer installed on your immune system

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — The brakes the cancer installed on your immune system
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.
For the next two hours we are going to talk about what is, without exaggeration, the most important development in cancer therapy of the last fifty years — possibly of the last hundred.
We are going to talk about immune checkpoint inhibitors.
And I want to begin not with a molecule, not with a drug, not even with a patient, but with one mental picture that I want you to carry through the whole lecture.
Picture your immune system as an enormous standing army.
Trillions of cells, organized into divisions and patrols and reconnaissance units, walking through every tissue of your body, every minute of every day, looking for trouble.

The most lethal soldiers in that army are the T cells — specifically the killer T cells, the CD8 T cells, the ones that, when they identify a sick cell, walk up to it and inject it with toxins and tell it to die.
Your T cells kill virally infected cells.
They kill aberrant cells.
And, importantly, they kill — or could kill — many cancer cells, every day, before those cancer cells ever grow large enough for you to notice.
That last fact is going to anchor this entire lecture, so let me say it again.
Cancers — most cancers — are arising and being eliminated by your immune system constantly, throughout your life.
The cancers that you eventually get diagnosed with are not the cancers that arose; they are the cancers that escaped.
They are the cells that somehow managed to grow in the presence of a vast immune army that should, by all rights, have killed them years ago.
So the question that immunology and oncology spent the better part of a century trying to answer is: how do they escape?
What is the trick?
How does a cell carrying mutated, abnormal proteins — a cell that looks, biochemically, like a freak — manage to grow into a tumor the size of a walnut in an immune-competent host?

And the answer, the answer that won the 2018 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, the answer that has rewritten the standard of care for melanoma and lung cancer and kidney cancer and bladder cancer and Hodgkin lymphoma and a dozen others, the answer is this.
Cancers learn to install brakes on your immune system.
Your T cells, like any well-designed killing machine, have safety features.
They have built-in brakes.

There are molecules on the surface of your T cells whose job is to dampen, slow, or shut down the killing program when the killing has gone on too long, or when the target turns out to be a normal cell, or when the inflammation is reaching dangerous levels.
These molecules are called immune checkpoints.
They exist because, without them, your T cells would run amok — they'd attack everything, they'd cause catastrophic autoimmunity, they'd kill you faster than any infection ever could.
The checkpoints are the safety mechanisms that keep the lethal army from turning on the city it is supposed to protect.
And cancers — and here is the punchline of the entire field — cancers have figured out how to press those brakes.
They have figured out how to display, on their own surfaces or to recruit other cells to display, the exact molecular signals that tell an approaching T cell: stand down.
Don't kill me.
I am not a threat.
The T cell, doing what it was designed to do, obeys.
The cancer survives.
The cancer grows.
So immune checkpoint inhibitors are drugs that do one thing, conceptually.
They remove the brakes that the cancer has installed on your immune system.

They block the brake-pressing molecules, the T cells stop receiving the stand-down signal, and the army that was already there, that had already found the cancer, that had already started its assault and then been told to halt — that army gets unleashed.
It finishes the job.
That is the entire field, in one sentence.
Everything we are going to discuss for the next two hours is built on that idea.
Now, there are two specific brakes — two specific checkpoint molecules — that matter more than any of the others, at least so far.
They were discovered, independently, by two scientists working on opposite sides of the world, who shared the 2018 Nobel Prize for their parallel and complementary work.

The first brake is called CTLA-4, and it was the discovery of James Allison, an American immunologist who realized in the mid-1990s that this molecule was acting as a brake on T-cell activation and that an antibody blocking it could let mice's immune systems destroy their own tumors.

The second brake is called PD-1, and it was the discovery of Tasuku Honjo, a Japanese immunologist who cloned the molecule in 1992 not even knowing what it did, and then spent the next decade working out that it, too, was a brake — but a different brake, acting in a different place at a different stage.
Together, CTLA-4 and PD-1 — and PD-1's partner molecule, PD-L1 — form the molecular core of modern cancer immunotherapy.

The drugs you will hear about in the news — ipilimumab, pembrolizumab, nivolumab, atezolizumab, durvalumab, the whole "-mab" parade — every single one of them is, at its heart, an antibody that blocks one of these two brakes.
That's it.
That's the trick.

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can do three things — one, explain to a friend without jargon what a checkpoint inhibitor is and why it works; two, distinguish CTLA-4 from PD-1 from PD-L1 and roughly remember which drugs hit which target; three, look at a cancer-news headline about a checkpoint inhibitor and have a real sense of what it means, what the biomarkers are, who is likely to respond, and what the toxicities are — then we will have done our job.
Everything else is detail hung onto that scaffold.
Let's build the scaffold.
